findings.summary: LLM-generated (potentially injected) code reaches host/container execution in one hop with no approval gate, no audit record, and unrestricted container network egress, forming a complete untrusted-input-to-RCE-and-exfiltration chain.
CodeExecutorAgent defaults approval_func to None so all model-generated code is auto-executed with no human-in-the-loop; the only safeguard is a runtime UserWarning, not an enforced gate.

LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor runs generated code directly on the host, and its docstring claims dangerous-command regex sanitization that does not exist anywhere in the code, giving operators a false sense of a guardrail.

No durable or structured audit trail of executions exists; the code-executor agent declares event_logger but never calls it, and executions surface only as ephemeral in-stream AgentChat events, leaving code execution with no accountability.

DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor creates its container with no network isolation (default bridge), so executed code has unrestricted outbound egress and can exfiltrate data despite the remit gating egress behind an allow-list.
The Docker container is created with no CPU or memory limits, allowing runaway or hostile generated code to exhaust host resources (denial-of-wallet / DoS).

The Docker container runs as root with no non-root user, read-only rootfs, or capability drop, weakening isolation and compounding host-takeover risk under the recommended mounted-Docker-socket pattern.

The LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor copies the full parent os.environ into the executed code's environment, exposing host credentials and secrets to generated code in violation of the remit's forbidden parent-environment access.

CodeExecutorAgent's sources filter defaults to None, so in a group chat it will extract and execute code blocks from any agent's message rather than only the paired code-generator, expanding the accepted trust scope.
The default execution image is a mutable tag (python:3-slim) that is auto-pulled at start with no digest pinning or integrity verification, exposing the sandbox to image-swap supply-chain tampering.
